1	# BRUTALWAV: Browser-Based Digital Audio Workstation
2	
3	## System Overview
4	
5	BRUTALWAV is a professional browser-based Digital Audio Workstation built on the Web Audio API. It provides multi-track audio and MIDI arrangement, a full-featured synthesizer, TR-808 drum machine (sample-based with tweakable parameters), effects processing, full mixer routing, modulation matrix, arpeggiator, and a piano roll editor -- all within a Neo-Brutalist interface that exposes its structural mechanics.
6	
7	**Target users**: Musicians, producers, and sound designers seeking a capable, browser-native production environment.
8	
9	**Tech stack**: React 19 + TypeScript (strict), Vite, Zustand (UI state), Web Audio API with native nodes for standard effects + AudioWorklet for custom DSP. Canvas/direct DOM for high-frequency rendering (metering, waveforms, piano roll).
10	
11	**Design direction**: Neo-Brutalist Expressionism with bold color accents. JetBrains Mono for data/values, system neo-grotesque for headings. 8px spatial grid. High-contrast black/white base with electric blue, hot pink, acid green, amber, and red accent palette.
12	
13	**Advisory profile**: `webapp` (data-dense, state-heavy, real-time interactive)
14	
15	**Build-great-things note**: All user-facing epics should invoke `/compound:build-great-things` during their work phase. This DAW requires excellence across all six build phases: Foundation (IA, state architecture), Structure (grid, responsive, component architecture), Craft (typography, color system, visual hierarchy), Motion & Interaction (micro-interactions, drag-and-drop, keyboard shortcuts), Performance & Polish (loading states, edge states, accessibility), and Launch (onboarding).
16	
17	---
18	
19	## EARS Requirements
20	
21	### R-UBI: Ubiquitous Requirements (always true)
22	
23	| ID | Requirement |
24	|----|-------------|
25	| R-UBI-01 | The system shall render all audio through the Web Audio API AudioContext |
26	| R-UBI-02 | The system shall use native Web Audio nodes (BiquadFilterNode, DynamicsCompressorNode, ConvolverNode, GainNode, etc.) for standard effects processing, and AudioWorklet processors for custom DSP (synth voices, custom envelopes, modulation routing). No ScriptProcessorNode. |
27	| R-UBI-03 | The system shall maintain separate audio-thread state and UI-thread state, communicating via lock-free mechanisms (MessagePort, SharedArrayBuffer) |
28	| R-UBI-04 | The system shall use sample-accurate scheduling via AudioContext.currentTime for all timed audio events |
29	| R-UBI-05 | The system shall preserve non-destructive editing: clips reference source audio without modifying originals |
30	| R-UBI-06 | The system shall apply the 8px spatial grid for all UI layout |
31	| R-UBI-07 | The system shall support undo/redo for all user-initiated edit operations via command pattern |
32	| R-UBI-08 | The system shall display real-time audio metering (peak + RMS) for all active tracks and master |
33	| R-UBI-09 | The system shall follow the Neo-Brutalist design system: JetBrains Mono for data, high-contrast palette, visible structural borders, exposed grid |
34	| R-UBI-10 | The system shall enforce TypeScript strict mode with noUncheckedIndexedAccess |
35	| R-UBI-11 | The system shall maintain all editable session state as a serializable JSON structure |
36	
37	### R-EVT: Event-Driven Requirements
38	
39	| ID | Trigger | Requirement |
40	|----|---------|-------------|
41	| R-EVT-01 | When the user presses Play | The system shall begin playback from the current cursor position, scheduling audio events using the look-ahead scheduler pattern |
42	| R-EVT-02 | When the user presses Stop | The system shall stop all audio playback, silence all outputs, and return the cursor to the last play-start position |
43	| R-EVT-03 | When the user presses Pause | The system shall suspend playback at the current position, retaining cursor position for resume |
44	| R-EVT-04 | When the user triggers a MIDI note (keyboard/mouse on piano roll or virtual keyboard) | The system shall allocate a synth voice, apply the current patch parameters (oscillators, filter, envelopes, LFOs), and route through the instrument track's signal chain within 10ms |
45	| R-EVT-05 | When the user imports an audio file (WAV/MP3/OGG/FLAC) | The system shall decode the file via AudioContext.decodeAudioData, store it in the media pool, and create a clip on the selected track |
46	| R-EVT-06 | When the user creates a new track | The system shall add a channel strip to the mixer (fader, pan, mute, solo, inserts, sends) and a lane in the arrangement view |
47	| R-EVT-07 | When the user adds an effect to a track insert slot | The system shall instantiate the corresponding AudioNode(s) and insert them into the track's audio graph chain |
48	| R-EVT-08 | When the user changes a synth parameter (oscillator type, filter cutoff, envelope, LFO rate, etc.) | The system shall apply the change in real-time via AudioParam scheduling with no audible glitch |
49	| R-EVT-09 | When the user clicks on the arrangement timeline | The system shall move the playback cursor to the clicked position, snapped to the current grid resolution |
50	| R-EVT-10 | When the user drags a clip on the arrangement | The system shall move the clip non-destructively, updating its timeline position and displaying ghost preview during drag |
51	| R-EVT-11 | When the user splits a clip | The system shall create two clips from the original, each referencing the same source audio with adjusted offsets |
52	| R-EVT-12 | When the user triggers a step on the 808 sequencer | The system shall play the corresponding drum sample (bass drum, snare, hi-hat, etc.) with user-adjustable parameters (tone, decay, tune, volume) via playback rate, amplitude envelope, and filter manipulation |
53	| R-EVT-13 | When the user saves the session | The system shall serialize the full session state to JSON and persist it to browser storage (IndexedDB) |
54	| R-EVT-14 | When the user initiates bounce/export | The system shall render the session to an audio buffer via OfflineAudioContext and offer download as WAV |
55	| R-EVT-15 | When the user activates the arpeggiator | The system shall auto-play held notes in the selected pattern (up/down/up-down/random) at the tempo-synced rate |
56	| R-EVT-16 | When the user adjusts tempo (BPM) | The system shall update the transport clock and reschedule all tempo-dependent events (metronome, arpeggiator, synced LFOs, step sequencer) |
57	| R-EVT-17 | When the user presses a keyboard shortcut | The system shall execute the mapped command without requiring mouse interaction |
58	
59	### R-STA: State-Driven Requirements
60	
61	| ID | State | Requirement |
62	|----|-------|-------------|
63	| R-STA-01 | While the transport is playing | The system shall advance the playback cursor, schedule audio events via the look-ahead scheduler, and update metering at 60fps |
64	| R-STA-02 | While a track is armed for recording | The system shall display the track in recording-ready state (hot pink accent) and monitor audio input if applicable |
65	| R-STA-03 | While loop mode is active | The system shall return the playback cursor to the loop start point when it reaches the loop end point, with seamless crossfade |
66	| R-STA-04 | While a track is soloed | The system shall mute all non-soloed tracks (solo-in-place behavior), except tracks with solo-isolate enabled |
67	| R-STA-05 | While the synthesizer is in legato mode | The system shall not re-trigger envelopes for overlapping notes, instead gliding pitch between notes |
68	| R-STA-06 | While the 808 sequencer is running | The system shall advance through the 16-step pattern at the current tempo, triggering drum sounds with accent and flam as programmed |
69	| R-STA-07 | While the arpeggiator latch is on | The system shall continue playing the arpeggio pattern even after all keys are released |
70	| R-STA-08 | While AudioContext is suspended (no user gesture yet) | The system shall display a clear prompt to click/interact to start audio, and resume the context on the first user gesture |
71	| R-STA-09 | While any parameter automation is active | The system shall apply automated parameter changes at sample-accurate timing, overriding manual knob positions |
72	
73	### R-UNW: Unwanted Behavior Requirements
74	
75	| ID | Condition | Requirement |
76	|----|-----------|-------------|
77	| R-UNW-01 | If audio output clips (exceeds 0dBFS) | The system shall activate the clip indicator on the affected channel and master, persisting until manually cleared |
78	| R-UNW-02 | If AudioWorklet processing exceeds the quantum budget (128 samples) | The system shall log the overrun event and continue processing without crashing |
79	| R-UNW-03 | If audio file decode fails | The system shall display an error with the file name and format, suggesting supported alternatives |
80	| R-UNW-04 | If browser tab loses focus during playback | The system shall continue audio playback uninterrupted (no throttling of audio thread) |
81	| R-UNW-05 | If the user attempts to create a feedback loop in routing | The system shall perform DFS cycle detection on the signal graph before applying any routing mutation, reject the command, and display a warning. This is a mandatory pre-condition for every addRoute, addSend, and addInsert operation. |
82	| R-UNW-06 | If IndexedDB storage is full during session save | The system shall notify the user and offer to export the session as a downloadable file |
83	| R-UNW-07 | If a synthesizer voice exceeds polyphony limit | The system shall steal the oldest or lowest-priority voice using the configured voice allocation strategy |
84	| R-UNW-08 | If the session JSON is malformed on load | The system shall display a recovery dialog and attempt to load as much of the session as possible |
85	
86	### R-OPT: Optional/Feature Requirements
87	
88	| ID | Feature | Requirement |
89	|----|---------|-------------|
90	| R-OPT-01 | Where MIDI input device is connected | The system shall accept MIDI note and CC messages, routing them to the selected instrument track |
91	| R-OPT-02 | Where the user enables sidechain compression | The system shall route the sidechain source signal to the compressor's detector input on the target track |
92	| R-OPT-03 | Where the user activates dark mode / light mode toggle | The system shall switch the full UI to the alternate Neo-Brutalist theme variant |
93	| R-OPT-04 | Where the user opens the modulation matrix | The system shall display all available modulation sources and destinations with drag-to-connect routing |
94	| R-OPT-05 | Where the user loads a preset | The system shall deserialize the preset JSON and apply all synthesizer/effect parameters atomically |
95	
96	---
97	
98	## Architecture Diagrams
99	
100	### C4 Context Diagram
101	
102	```mermaid
103	C4Context
104	    title BRUTALWAV System Context
105	
106	    Person(musician, "Musician / Producer", "Creates music using the DAW")
107	
108	    System(brutalwav, "BRUTALWAV", "Browser-based DAW with synth, 808, mixer, effects, piano roll, arrangement")
109	
110	    System_Ext(webaudio, "Web Audio API", "Browser audio engine: AudioContext, AudioWorklet, AudioNodes")
111	    System_Ext(indexeddb, "IndexedDB", "Browser persistent storage for sessions and audio files")
112	    System_Ext(midi, "Web MIDI API", "External MIDI controller input")
113	    System_Ext(filesystem, "File System Access API", "Import/export audio files and sessions")
114	
115	    Rel(musician, brutalwav, "Composes, arranges, mixes, exports")
116	    Rel(brutalwav, webaudio, "Creates audio graph, schedules events, renders audio")
117	    Rel(brutalwav, indexeddb, "Persists sessions, presets, media pool")
118	    Rel(brutalwav, midi, "Receives MIDI note/CC input")
119	    Rel(brutalwav, filesystem, "Imports audio, exports WAV/project")
120	```
121	
122	### C4 Container Diagram
123	
124	```mermaid
125	C4Context
126	    title BRUTALWAV Container Architecture
127	
128	    Container_Boundary(browser, "Browser") {
129	        Container(ui, "UI Layer", "React 19 + TypeScript", "Arrangement, mixer, piano roll, synth editor, 808 pads")
130	        Container(state, "State Manager", "Zustand", "UI state, session model, undo history")
131	        Container(engine, "Audio Engine", "TypeScript + AudioWorklet", "Audio graph, scheduling, DSP processing")
132	        Container(transport, "Transport Controller", "TypeScript", "Play/pause/stop/loop, tempo map, cursor sync")
133	        Container(synth, "Synthesizer Core", "AudioWorklet", "Oscillators, filters, envelopes, LFOs, modulation matrix")
134	        Container(drums, "808 Drum Machine", "AudioWorklet + WebAudio nodes", "Analog-modeled drum synthesis + step sequencer")
135	        Container(effects, "Effects Rack", "WebAudio nodes + AudioWorklet", "Reverb, delay, compressor, EQ, distortion, chorus")
136	        Container(mixer, "Mixer Engine", "TypeScript + WebAudio", "Channel strips, routing, faders, pan, sends, master bus")
137	        Container(media, "Media Pool", "TypeScript + IndexedDB", "Audio file storage, decode, reference management")
138	    }
139	
140	    Rel(ui, state, "Reads/dispatches")
141	    Rel(state, engine, "Commands via MessagePort")
142	    Rel(engine, transport, "Clock sync")
143	    Rel(engine, synth, "Note events")
144	    Rel(engine, drums, "Trigger events")
145	    Rel(engine, effects, "Signal routing")
146	    Rel(engine, mixer, "Channel routing")
147	    Rel(ui, media, "Import/browse files")
148	```
149	
150	### Transport State Diagram
151	
152	```mermaid
153	stateDiagram-v2
154	    [*] --> Stopped
155	    Stopped --> Playing : play()
156	    Stopped --> Stopped : stop() [already stopped]
157	    Playing --> Paused : pause()
158	    Playing --> Stopped : stop()
159	    Playing --> Playing : seek() [reposition cursor]
160	    Paused --> Playing : play() [resume from position]
161	    Paused --> Stopped : stop()
162	    Paused --> Paused : seek()
163	
164	    state Playing {
165	        [*] --> NormalPlay
166	        NormalPlay --> LoopPlay : enableLoop()
167	        LoopPlay --> NormalPlay : disableLoop()
168	        LoopPlay --> LoopPlay : reachLoopEnd [jump to loop start]
169	    }
170	```
171	
172	### Synthesizer Voice Lifecycle
173	
174	```mermaid
175	stateDiagram-v2
176	    [*] --> Idle
177	    Idle --> Attack : noteOn(pitch, velocity)
178	    Attack --> Decay : attackComplete
179	    Decay --> Sustain : decayComplete
180	    Sustain --> Release : noteOff()
181	    Release --> Idle : releaseComplete [voice freed]
182	
183	    Attack --> Release : noteOff() [short note]
184	    Decay --> Release : noteOff() [medium note]
185	
186	    Idle --> Stolen : voiceSteal [polyphony limit]
187	    Stolen --> Attack : noteOn(newPitch) [reassigned]
188	```
189	
190	### Audio Signal Flow Sequence
191	
192	```mermaid
193	sequenceDiagram
194	    participant User
195	    participant UI
196	    participant Transport
197	    participant Scheduler
198	    participant AudioGraph
199	    participant Mixer
200	    participant Master
201	    participant Speakers
202	
203	    User->>UI: Press Play
204	    UI->>Transport: play()
205	    Transport->>Scheduler: start look-ahead loop
206	    loop Every 25ms (look-ahead)
207	        Scheduler->>Scheduler: Check events in next 100ms window
208	        Scheduler->>AudioGraph: Schedule source.start(time), noteOn(time)
209	    end
210	    AudioGraph->>Mixer: Per-track audio signal
211	    Mixer->>Mixer: Apply inserts, fader, pan, sends
212	    Mixer->>Master: Sum to master bus
213	    Master->>Speakers: AudioContext.destination
214	    Transport->>UI: Cursor position update (rAF)
215	```
216	
217	### 808 Step Sequencer Flow
218	
219	```mermaid
220	sequenceDiagram
221	    participant Sequencer
222	    participant Transport
223	    participant Scheduler
224	    participant DrumVoice
225	    participant Mixer
226	
227	    Transport->>Sequencer: Beat clock tick
228	    Sequencer->>Sequencer: Advance to next step (0-15)
229	    Sequencer->>Scheduler: Read pattern: which drums active on this step
230	    loop For each active drum on step
231	        Scheduler->>DrumVoice: triggerDrum(type, accent, time)
232	        DrumVoice->>DrumVoice: Synthesize (oscillator + noise + filter + envelope)
233	        DrumVoice->>Mixer: Audio output to drum bus
234	    end
235	```
236	
237	---
238	
239	## Scenario Table
240	
241	| # | Scenario | EARS Refs | Trigger | Expected Behavior | Verification |
242	|---|----------|-----------|---------|-------------------|-------------|
243	| S01 | Basic playback | R-EVT-01, R-STA-01 | User presses Space (Play) | Audio plays from cursor, cursor advances, meters move | Audio output matches expected waveform via OfflineAudioContext |
244	| S02 | Stop and return | R-EVT-02 | User presses Space (Stop) while playing | Audio stops, cursor returns to start position | Transport state = Stopped, cursor = last start |
245	| S03 | Loop playback | R-STA-03 | Enable loop region, press Play | Seamless loop at boundaries | Rendered audio shows seamless transition at loop point |
246	| S04 | Synth note trigger | R-EVT-04 | Press key on virtual keyboard | Note sounds within 10ms, ADSR envelope shapes amplitude | FFT confirms correct pitch, amplitude envelope matches ADSR |
247	| S05 | 808 bass drum | R-EVT-12 | Trigger bass drum step | Characteristic 808 kick: sub-frequency punch with pitch sweep | Spectral analysis shows fundamental < 80Hz with descending pitch envelope |
248	| S06 | 808 pattern play | R-STA-06 | Activate 808 sequencer | 16-step pattern advances at tempo | Drum hits land on expected beat positions (sample-accurate) |
249	| S07 | Import audio | R-EVT-05 | Drag WAV file into arrangement | File decodes, clip appears on track | Decoded buffer matches source file, clip visible in UI |
250	| S08 | Add effect | R-EVT-07 | Add reverb to track insert | Audio passes through reverb processing | Wet/dry comparison shows reverb tail in output |
251	| S09 | Filter sweep | R-EVT-08 | Turn filter cutoff knob on synth | Real-time frequency change, no glitch | Spectrogram shows smooth frequency transition |
252	| S10 | Clip split | R-EVT-11 | Split clip at cursor | Two clips, same source, adjusted offsets | Both clips play identical content to original at their positions |
253	| S11 | Solo track | R-STA-04 | Solo track 2 | Only track 2 audible | Master output = track 2 output (within float precision) |
254	| S12 | Arpeggiator | R-EVT-15, R-STA-07 | Enable arp, hold chord | Notes play in pattern at tempo rate | Note timing matches expected pattern at BPM |
255	| S13 | Polyphony limit | R-UNW-07 | Play more notes than max polyphony | Oldest voice stolen, no silence gap | Continuous audio output, stolen voice fades |
256	| S14 | Clip indicator | R-UNW-01 | Push fader above 0dB with hot signal | Clip LED lights, stays lit | Peak meter shows clip, indicator persists |
257	| S15 | AudioContext resume | R-STA-08 | Open app fresh (no gesture) | Shows "Click to start audio" | AudioContext state = suspended until click |
258	| S16 | Feedback prevention | R-UNW-05 | Route track send to itself | Connection rejected, warning shown | Audio graph has no cycles |
259	| S17 | Session save/load | R-EVT-13, R-UNW-08 | Save session, reload page, load | Session restores identically | All tracks, clips, parameters match saved state |
260	| S18 | Bounce to WAV | R-EVT-14 | Select bounce, choose range | WAV file offered for download | Downloaded WAV matches real-time playback |
261	| S19 | Tempo change | R-EVT-16 | Change BPM from 120 to 140 | All tempo-synced elements update | Metronome, arp, LFO rates recalculate correctly |
262	| S20 | MIDI input | R-OPT-01 | Play note on MIDI controller | Note triggers on selected instrument | Same as S04, triggered via MIDI instead of mouse |
263	| S21 | Sidechain duck | R-OPT-02 | Setup sidechain: kick -> bass compressor | Bass ducks on kick hits | Amplitude analysis shows bass reduction coinciding with kick |
264	| S22 | Undo/redo | R-UBI-07 | Delete clip, then Ctrl+Z | Clip restored | Session state matches pre-delete state |
265	| S23 | Keyboard shortcuts | R-EVT-17 | Press 'M' on track | Track mutes | Track output = silence |
266	
267	---
268	
269	## Non-Functional Requirements
270	
271	| ID | Category | Requirement |
272	|----|----------|-------------|
273	| NFR-01 | Latency | Audio output latency shall be < 20ms from event trigger to audible output |
274	| NFR-02 | Performance | The system shall sustain 16 simultaneous tracks with effects at 60fps UI update rate |
275	| NFR-03 | Performance | Synthesizer shall support 16-voice polyphony without audio glitches |
276	| NFR-04 | Accessibility | All interactive controls shall be keyboard-navigable (WCAG 2.2 AA) |
277	| NFR-05 | Accessibility | prefers-reduced-motion shall be respected for all UI animations |
278	| NFR-06 | Browser Support | Chrome 90+, Firefox 90+, Safari 16+ (AudioWorklet baseline) |
279	| NFR-07 | Responsiveness | Minimum viewport: 1024px width (desktop-focused tool) |
280	| NFR-08 | Storage | Sessions up to 50 tracks shall save/load within 2 seconds |
281	| NFR-09 | Audio Quality | All DSP processing shall use 32-bit float at the AudioContext's native sample rate |
282	| NFR-10 | Testability | All DSP algorithms shall be testable via OfflineAudioContext (deterministic rendering) |
283	| NFR-11 | Deployment | The application server shall set Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin and Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy: require-corp headers. App shall check crossOriginIsolated === true at startup and display hard error if false. |
284	| NFR-12 | Memory | Audio files shall use chunked streaming from IndexedDB/File System Access API. AudioBuffer used only for short samples (<30s). Long tracks use streaming or MediaElementAudioSourceNode. Imported file size limit: 500MB. |
285	| NFR-13 | Rendering | High-frequency UI updates (metering, waveforms, playback cursor, piano roll) shall bypass React state and render directly to HTML5 Canvas or mutate DOM via useRef, reading from SharedArrayBuffer via requestAnimationFrame. |
286	| NFR-14 | Audio Safety | AudioWorklet process() methods shall follow zero-allocation coding: all buffers pre-allocated at initialization, no object/array creation in the render loop. |
287	| NFR-15 | Data Integrity | Session JSON shall be validated against a Zod schema on load. Schema includes version field. Recovery loads valid portions and discards invalid data. Write uses draft-then-swap pattern with backup. |
288	| NFR-16 | Input Validation | All MIDI input values shall be clamped to valid ranges (note/CC: 0-127, pitch bend: 0-16383). SysEx messages capped at 1KB. All external input treated as untrusted. |
289	| NFR-17 | Export | Bounce/export shall use chunked OfflineAudioContext rendering (30-second blocks) with incremental WAV encoding to avoid single large buffer allocation. |

1	# Advisory Fleet Brief: BRUTALWAV
2	
3	**Advisors consulted**: Security & Reliability (Claude Sonnet), Scalability & Performance (Gemini), Simplicity & Alternatives (Claude Opus)
4	**Advisors unavailable**: Organizational & Delivery (Codex -- timed out during execution)
5	
6	---
7	
8	## P0 Concerns
9	
10	### 1. SharedArrayBuffer requires cross-origin isolation headers (Security)
11	The lock-free audio/UI communication via SharedArrayBuffer silently fails without `Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin` + `Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy: require-corp`. The spec treats SAB as a given but this is a hard deployment constraint.
12	- **Action**: Promote to NFR. Add startup check: `crossOriginIsolated === true` or hard error. Document required server headers.
13	
14	### 2. Memory exhaustion via fully-decoded AudioBuffers (Scalability)
15	A 5-minute stereo track at 44.1kHz/32-bit = ~105MB. 50 tracks = ~5.2GB. Browser tabs will crash.
16	- **Action**: Implement chunked streaming from IndexedDB/File System Access API. Use AudioBuffer only for short samples. Consider MediaElementAudioSourceNode for long backing tracks.
17	
18	### 3. React re-render thrashing for 60fps metering (Scalability)
19	Feeding 60fps metering through Zustand/React state will cause massive render tree updates.
20	- **Action**: Bypass React state for high-frequency updates. Use requestAnimationFrame to read directly from SharedArrayBuffer and mutate raw DOM/canvas via useRef.
21	
22	### 4. Feedback loop detection has no implementation contract (Security)
23	R-UNW-05 names the requirement but gives no implementation. Cycles cause runaway gain -- speaker/hearing damage.
24	- **Action**: Mandatory DFS cycle detection on every routing mutation. Reject the command before AudioNode connection is made.
25	
26	### 5. Full analog-modeled 808 from scratch is the biggest complexity risk (Simplicity)
27	11 drum instruments, each a mini-synthesizer with AudioWorklet. 3-4x effort of sample-based drums with marginal perceptible benefit.
28	- **Action**: Ship with high-quality 808 samples + Tone/Decay/Tune knobs for tweakability. Make analog modeling a future epic.
29	
30	### 6. "AudioWorklet for ALL DSP" is an over-constraint (Simplicity)
31	Native nodes (BiquadFilterNode, DynamicsCompressorNode, ConvolverNode, etc.) are C++ implementations -- faster and more battle-tested than JS AudioWorklet equivalents.
32	- **Action**: Relax R-UBI-02. Use native Web Audio nodes where they suffice. Reserve AudioWorklet for custom DSP only (synth voices, custom envelopes, modulation).
33	
34	---
35	
36	## P1 Concerns
37	
38	### 7. AudioWorklet GC pauses will cause audio dropouts (Scalability)
39	Memory allocation in the process() loop triggers GC, causing immediate audio artifacts.
40	- **Action**: Mandate zero-allocation coding in AudioWorklet process(). Pre-allocate all buffers during initialization. Consider WASM for heavy DSP.
41	
42	### 8. Decoded audio buffer size is unbounded (Security)
43	A 192kHz/32-bit/8-channel 60-minute FLAC = ~25GB decoded. DoS vector.
44	- **Action**: File size limit + estimated decoded size check before decodeAudioData. Reject oversized buffers.
45	
46	### 9. Session JSON has no schema validation (Security)
47	JSON.parse of arbitrary input risks prototype pollution. Crafted sessions could produce invalid DSP topology.
48	- **Action**: Validate with Zod schema. Include schemaVersion. Recovery = load what validates, discard the rest.
49	
50	### 10. OfflineAudioContext bounce is unbounded in memory (Security)
51	10-minute stereo at 48kHz = ~230MB for the raw buffer alone.
52	- **Action**: Chunked render strategy (30-second blocks), stream to FileSystemWritableFileStream.
53	
54	### 11. MIDI input has no validation boundary (Security)
55	SysEx floods, out-of-range CC values passed to AudioParam could produce NaN/Infinity.
56	- **Action**: MIDI input validation layer: clamp all values, enforce max SysEx length, treat all MIDI as untrusted.
57	
58	### 12. Modulation matrix is premature for v1 (Simplicity)
59	Full drag-to-connect routing is a major UI/DSP subsystem. Popular synths ship without it.
60	- **Action**: Hardwire standard modulation paths (filter env -> cutoff, LFO -> pitch/cutoff/amp, velocity -> filter/amp). Matrix deferred to later.
61	
62	### 13. Full mixer routing (sidechain, sends, buses) on day one is scope creep (Simplicity)
63	Requires bus architecture, cycle detection, topological sort updates on every change.
64	- **Action**: v1 ships insert-only effects + master bus. Add sends/buses/sidechain as later epic.
65	
66	### 14. Custom design system from scratch is high-effort, low-leverage (Simplicity)
67	Building every UI primitive (buttons, sliders, dropdowns, modals) from scratch absorbs 20-30% of UI time.
68	- **Action**: Start with shadcn/ui (Radix primitives) + brutalist theme. Custom-build only DAW-specific controls (knobs, faders, meters, piano roll).
69	
70	### 15. 50-track session load < 2s is unrealistic without lazy loading (Scalability)
71	decodeAudioData is CPU-bound. 50 files takes >>2 seconds.
72	- **Action**: Lazy load. Persist pre-computed waveform peaks in IndexedDB. Render UI instantly, decode audio in background.
73	
74	### 16. AudioWorklet quantum overrun has no degradation contract (Security)
75	Spec says "log and continue" but doesn't define what "continue" means.
76	- **Action**: Define: emit silence for that quantum, post overrun message, mute track after N consecutive overruns.
77	
78	---
79	
80	## P2 Concerns
81	
82	### 17. DOM overload in piano roll and arrangement (Scalability)
83	Thousands of MIDI notes and waveform SVGs will overwhelm the layout engine.
84	- **Action**: Use HTML5 Canvas or WebGL for timeline, waveforms, and piano roll.
85	
86	### 18. Look-ahead scheduler drift from main-thread blocking (Scalability)
87	setInterval on UI thread is vulnerable to React rendering blocks.
88	- **Action**: Move scheduler to a Web Worker or manage timing inside AudioWorklet.
89	
90	### 19. Both convolution AND algorithmic reverb is redundant (Simplicity)
91	Two implementations, double the testing.
92	- **Action**: Ship ConvolverNode reverb only for v1. Add algorithmic later.
93	
94	### 20. Arpeggiator is nice-to-have, not core (Simplicity)
95	Self-contained feature that doesn't block any workflow.
96	- **Action**: Defer. Piano roll covers manual arpeggio patterns.
97	
98	### 21. IndexedDB writes aren't transactionally safe (Security)
99	Browser crash mid-write = corrupt session.
100	- **Action**: Write-then-swap pattern with session_draft -> session_current + session_backup.
101	
102	### 22. Undo stack holds unbounded buffer references (Security)
103	Long sessions accumulate hundreds of commands holding AudioBuffer references.
104	- **Action**: Max undo depth (200), buffer references as IDs into separate cache with eviction.
105	
106	### 23. Dual-state pattern over-applied (Simplicity)
107	Not all state needs lock-free communication. Session metadata is UI-only.
108	- **Action**: Dual-state only for audio-thread-relevant state (transport, params, graph topology). Keep rest in Zustand.
109	
110	### 24. File import lacks magic-bytes validation (Security)
111	Extension-based validation is trivially bypassed.
112	- **Action**: Check first 12 bytes for WAV/MP3/OGG/FLAC signatures before decodeAudioData.
113	
114	---
115	
116	## Strengths (Consensus)
117	
118	All three advisors agreed on these architectural strengths:
119	
120	1. **AudioWorklet thread isolation** is the correct architecture for browser DSP (all three)
121	2. **Look-ahead scheduler pattern** is the only way to get sample-accurate timing (Scalability, Simplicity)
122	3. **Non-destructive editing** with clip references eliminates corruption bugs (Security, Scalability)
123	4. **React 19 + Zustand** is appropriately simple -- Tone.js abstraction would fight custom DSP (Simplicity)
124	5. **OfflineAudioContext for bounce/export** is the standard, correct approach (Scalability, Simplicity)
125	6. **IndexedDB persistence** without server complexity is pragmatic (Simplicity)
126	7. **Command-pattern undo/redo** is the right architecture for heterogeneous edit operations (Security, Simplicity)
127	8. **Desktop-only (1024px min)** is wise -- DAWs are desktop tools (Simplicity)
128	9. **Strict TypeScript + 32-bit float** reduces coercion bugs at AudioParam boundaries (Security)
129	10. **16-voice polyphony ceiling** matches browser capability (Simplicity)
130	
131	---
132	
133	## Alternative Approaches
134	
135	**From Simplicity advisor (Claude Opus, HIGH confidence):**
136	
137	The "simplest viable DAW" that still delivers a real product:
138	- Sample-based 808 with tweakable parameters (not analog-modeled synthesis)
139	- Native Web Audio nodes for standard effects
140	- Insert-only effects chain (no sends/buses/sidechain v1)
141	- Hardwired synth modulation paths (not full matrix)
142	- shadcn/ui with brutalist theme (not from-scratch design system)
143	- Convolution reverb only (not dual implementations)
144	- No arpeggiator
145	
146	This still delivers: synthesizer with PolyBLEP oscillators + filters + ADSR + LFOs, 808 patterns with tweakable samples, effects, mixer with faders/inserts, piano roll, arrangement, save/load, bounce.
147	
148	---
149	
150	## Confidence Summary
151	
152	| Advisor | Confidence | Justification |
153	|---------|-----------|---------------|
154	| Security & Reliability | MEDIUM | Right requirements named, but cross-origin isolation absent, degradation contracts underspecified, trust model implicit |
155	| Scalability & Performance | HIGH | Browser DAW limitations well-documented; concerns directly address physical limits of memory, GC, and rendering |
156	| Organizational & Delivery | N/A | Codex timed out |
157	| Simplicity & Alternatives | HIGH | Spec is well-researched but bundles v1 and v3 features; core architecture is sound, risk is in scope size |

1	# Project Instructions
2	<!-- compound-agent:claude-ref:start -->
3	## Compound Agent
4	See AGENTS.md for lesson capture workflow.
5	<!-- compound-agent:claude-ref:end -->
6	
7	You have a lot of research that you can scheme in @docs/. Do not hesitate to send subagents there when needed.
8	
9	## Agentic Codebase Principles
10	
11	This project follows the **Agentic Codebase Manifesto** (15 principles, 3 pillars). The full audit skill lives in `.claude/skills/compound/agentic/SKILL.md`.
12	
13	### Linting & Constraints (P6)
14	
15	Tight linting is a force multiplier for agentic development:
16	
17	- **Strict TypeScript** (`strict`, `noUncheckedIndexedAccess`, `exactOptionalPropertyTypes`) -- catches type errors at compile time, not runtime.
18	- **ESLint strict type-checked** with custom architectural rules -- import boundaries enforced mechanically (no deep relative imports, use `@/` aliases).
19	- **Pre-commit hooks + CI gates** -- linting that isn't enforced is linting that drifts. Both Husky and GitHub Actions enforce the same `pnpm check`.
20	- **Custom lint rules** for project-specific constraints (import boundaries, naming conventions, forbidden patterns). These make the codebase self-documenting for agents.
21	
22	### Key Manifesto Concepts
23	
24	- **Repository is the only truth** (P1): All context an agent needs lives in version control
25	- **Trace decisions** (P2): Use ADRs in `docs/adr/` for architectural rationale
26	- **Constraints are multipliers** (P6): Linters, type checkers, and custom rules reduce agent error surface
27	- **Write feedback for machines** (P7): Structured errors with remediation hints
28	- **Map, not manual** (P9): AGENTS.md provides navigable entry points, not encyclopedias
29	- **Simplicity compounds** (P13): Prefer boring tech, minimal abstractions

1	/**
2	 * SharedArrayBuffer byte layout for lock-free audio-thread -> UI-thread communication.
3	 * All offsets are in bytes. Float32 values use 4-byte alignment.
4	 *
5	 * INV-3: Zero-allocation convention -- all buffers pre-allocated at init,
6	 * no object/array creation in the render loop.
7	 */
8	
9	/** Byte offsets into the shared metering buffer */
10	export const MeteringLayout = {
11	  /** Master peak L (Float32) */
12	  MASTER_PEAK_L: 0,
13	  /** Master peak R (Float32) */
14	  MASTER_PEAK_R: 4,
15	  /** Master RMS L (Float32) */
16	  MASTER_RMS_L: 8,
17	  /** Master RMS R (Float32) */
18	  MASTER_RMS_R: 12,
19	  /** Per-track metering starts here. Each track: peakL, peakR, rmsL, rmsR (16 bytes) */
20	  TRACKS_OFFSET: 16,
21	  /** Bytes per track metering block */
22	  BYTES_PER_TRACK: 16,
23	} as const;
24	
25	/**
26	 * Byte offsets into the shared transport/cursor buffer.
27	 *
28	 * Note on atomics: Float32/Float64 views cannot use Atomics directly.
29	 * CURSOR_SECONDS and BPM must be read/written via integer-typed views
30	 * (e.g., Int32Array for BPM, BigInt64Array for CURSOR_SECONDS) or
31	 * accept non-atomic access with appropriate fencing.
32	 */
33	export const TransportLayout = {
34	  /** Playback cursor position in seconds (Float64, 8 bytes) */
35	  CURSOR_SECONDS: 0,
36	  /** Transport state: 0=stopped, 1=playing, 2=paused (Uint8) */
37	  STATE: 8,
38	  // Bytes 9-11: alignment padding (reserved, do not use)
39	  /** Current BPM (Float32, 4-byte aligned) */
40	  BPM: 12,
41	  /** Total size of the transport buffer */
42	  TOTAL_BYTES: 16,
43	} as const;
44	
45	export type SharedArrayBufferLayout = {
46	  metering: SharedArrayBuffer;
47	  transport: SharedArrayBuffer;
48	};
49	
50	/**
51	 * Creates the shared buffers for audio-thread <-> UI-thread communication.
52	 * @param maxTracks - Maximum number of tracks supported (default 64)
53	 */
54	export function createSharedBuffers(maxTracks = 64): SharedArrayBufferLayout {
55	  const meteringSize =
56	    MeteringLayout.TRACKS_OFFSET + maxTracks * MeteringLayout.BYTES_PER_TRACK;
57	  return {
58	    metering: new SharedArrayBuffer(meteringSize),
59	    transport: new SharedArrayBuffer(TransportLayout.TOTAL_BYTES),
60	  };
61	}

2	title: "Swiss Brutalist Design: A Comprehensive Survey"
3	date: 2026-02-22
4	summary: "Examines Swiss Brutalist Design at the intersection of Swiss International Typographic Style and Brutalism—tracing lineage from Emil Ruder and Muller-Brockmann through the Smithsons' architectural \"as found\" material honesty to contemporary syntheses by Experimental Jetset and Wolfgang Weingart. Catalogs specific typographic, chromatic, spatial, and compositional parameters across four sub-approaches: Orthodox Swiss Brutalism, Neo-Brutalist Expressionism, Systematic Minimalism, and Typographic Brutalism."
5	keywords: [design, swiss-brutalism, brutalism, typography, graphic-design]
6	---
7	
8	# Swiss Brutalist Design: A Comprehensive Survey
9	*2026-02-22*
10	
11	---
12	
13	## Abstract
14	
15	Swiss Brutalist Design occupies a distinctive position at the intersection of two of the twentieth century's most consequential design movements: the Swiss International Typographic Style (also known as the International Style or Swiss Style) and Brutalism, which originated in post-war architecture before migrating into graphic design, digital interfaces, and broader visual culture. This survey examines the theoretical foundations, taxonomic classification, and precise design specifications of Swiss Brutalist Design as a coherent aesthetic and philosophical system. It traces the lineage from the rationalist grid systems of Emil Ruder and Josef Muller-Brockmann through the "as found" material honesty of Alison and Peter Smithson's architectural Brutalism, arriving at a contemporary synthesis that privileges structural transparency, typographic rawness, and systematic reduction.
16	
17	The document provides a rigorous academic treatment of the movement's philosophical underpinnings, drawing on primary sources including manifestos, pedagogical texts from the Basel School of Design and the Zurich School of Arts, and critical writings from practitioners such as Experimental Jetset, Karl Gerstner, and Wolfgang Weingart. It catalogs the specific typographic, chromatic, spatial, and compositional parameters that define Swiss Brutalist work, offering exact specifications (font families, type scales, grid measurements, color values) suitable for direct application. The taxonomy presented here distinguishes among several sub-approaches -- Orthodox Swiss Brutalism, Neo-Brutalist Expressionism, Systematic Minimalism, and Typographic Brutalism -- each analyzed with reference to theory, practitioners, and critical reception.
18	
19	This survey is intended as a standalone, project-agnostic reference document. It synthesizes design history, critical theory, and technical specification without prescribing implementation in any particular medium. The goal is to provide a definitive scholarly resource on Swiss Brutalist Design as both an intellectual tradition and a practical design system.
20	
21	---
22	
23	## 1. Introduction
24	
25	### 1.1 Problem Statement
26	
27	Despite the widespread adoption of "brutalist" aesthetics in contemporary graphic and digital design, the term remains poorly defined in academic literature. Popular usage conflates several distinct traditions: the architectural Brutalism of Le Corbusier and the Smithsons, the post-punk graphic design of Neville Brody and David Carson, the systematic rationalism of Swiss typography, and the deliberately crude digital aesthetics of early web nostalgia. This conflation obscures meaningful distinctions and impedes rigorous analysis.
28	
29	Swiss Brutalist Design, as treated in this survey, refers specifically to design work that synthesizes the structural rigor and typographic discipline of the Swiss International Typographic Style with the material honesty, exposed-structure philosophy, and deliberate anti-refinement of architectural Brutalism. It is not merely "ugly design" or "raw design" but a principled approach rooted in transparency of process, truth to materials, and the primacy of systematic order over decorative embellishment.
30	
31	### 1.2 Scope
32	
33	This document addresses:
34	
35	- The historical origins and philosophical foundations of Swiss Brutalist Design
36	- A taxonomic classification of approaches within the movement
37	- Detailed analysis of each approach, including key practitioners and critical assessment
38	- Precise design specifications across typography, color, grid systems, and visual elements
39	- Comparative synthesis and identification of open research questions
40	
41	This document does not address:
42	
43	- Code implementation, CSS frameworks, or web development techniques
44	- Project-specific application guidance
45	- Commercial branding strategy or market positioning
46	
47	### 1.3 Definitions
48	
49	**Brutalism (architectural):** A post-war architectural movement characterized by raw, unfinished surfaces (particularly beton brut, or raw concrete), monolithic forms, exposure of structural elements, and a rejection of decorative concealment. Originated in 1950s Britain with the Smithsons and consolidated by Reyner Banham's critical writings.
50	
51	**Swiss International Typographic Style:** A graphic design movement originating in 1950s Switzerland (primarily Basel and Zurich), characterized by mathematical grid systems, sans-serif typography (particularly Akzidenz-Grotesk and later Helvetica), asymmetric layouts, objective photography over illustration, and a commitment to "universal" visual communication.
52	
53	**Swiss Brutalist Design:** The synthesis of these two traditions: work that employs Swiss typographic systematization while embracing the rawness, structural exposure, and anti-decorative stance of Brutalism. It foregrounds the mechanics of design (grids, type specimens, raw data) rather than concealing them behind polished surfaces.
54	
55	**Neo-Brutalism:** A contemporary reinterpretation (c. 2014-present) that applies brutalist principles to digital interfaces, often characterized by visible borders, system fonts, monospaced type, high-contrast color, and deliberate rejection of skeuomorphic or "polished" aesthetics.
56	
57	---
58	
59	## 2. Foundations
60	
61	### 2.1 Historical Origins
62	
63	#### 2.1.1 Architectural Brutalism (1950-1975)
64	
65	The term "Brutalism" derives from the French beton brut (raw concrete), used by Le Corbusier to describe the exposed concrete surfaces of his Unite d'Habitation in Marseille (1947-1952). The term was adopted and theorized by British architects Alison and Peter Smithson, whose Hunstanton School (1949-1954) exemplified what they called "the New Brutalism" -- an architecture in which all structural and service elements are exposed and celebrated rather than concealed.
66	
67	Reyner Banham, the movement's most important critic, codified its principles in "The New Brutalism: Ethic or Aesthetic?" (1966), arguing that Brutalism was defined by three characteristics:
68	
69	1. **Memorability as image:** The building must make an immediate, powerful visual impression
70	2. **Clear exhibition of structure:** Load-bearing and service elements are visible
71	3. **Valuation of materials "as found":** Materials are presented in their raw state without applied finishes
72	
73	This triad -- memorability, structural transparency, material honesty -- provides the philosophical foundation for all subsequent brutalist design, including its graphic manifestations.
74	
75	#### 2.1.2 The Swiss Typographic Tradition (1927-1970)
76	
77	The Swiss International Typographic Style crystallized in the 1950s but drew on earlier developments:
78	
79	- **Jan Tschichold's "Die Neue Typographie" (1928):** Established principles of asymmetric layout, sans-serif type, and functional hierarchy that would define Swiss design
80	- **Theo Ballmer and Max Bill:** Bridged Bauhaus constructivism and Swiss graphic design in the 1930s-1940s
81	- **Ernst Keller** at the Zurich Kunstgewerbeschule: Trained a generation of Swiss designers from the 1920s onward in systematic, craft-oriented approaches
82	- **Basel School of Design (Allgemeine Gewerbeschule Basel):** Under Armin Hofmann and Emil Ruder from the 1940s, became the primary institutional home of Swiss typography
83	- **Zurich School (Kunstgewerbeschule Zurich):** Under Josef Muller-Brockmann, developed the mathematical grid system approach
84	
85	Key publications that codified the style include:
86	
87	- Emil Ruder, *Typographie: A Manual of Design* (1967)
88	- Josef Muller-Brockmann, *Grid Systems in Graphic Design* (1981)
89	- Karl Gerstner, *Designing Programmes* (1964)
90	- The journal *Neue Grafik* (1958-1965), edited by Muller-Brockmann, Richard Paul Lohse, Hans Neuburg, and Carlo Vivarelli
91	
92	#### 2.1.3 The Migration from Architecture to Graphic Design (1970-2000)
93	
94	The transfer of brutalist principles from architecture to graphic design occurred through several channels:
95	
96	**Wolfgang Weingart and the "New Wave" (1968-1980s):** As a teacher at the Basel School of Design, Weingart began deconstructing Swiss typographic conventions from within, introducing layered type, extreme letterspacing, mixed weights, and deliberate disruption of the grid. His work retained the systematic foundation of Swiss design while exposing and subverting its mechanics -- a direct parallel to architectural Brutalism's exposure of structure.
97	
98	**Punk and Post-Punk Graphics (1976-1985):** Jamie Reid's work for the Sex Pistols and Neville Brody's art direction for *The Face* magazine introduced raw, collaged, deliberately "undesigned" aesthetics that shared Brutalism's anti-decorative stance, though from a countercultural rather than rationalist motivation.
99	
100	**David Carson and "The End of Print" (1990s):** Carson's work for *Ray Gun* magazine pushed graphic rawness to an extreme, deliberately sacrificing legibility for expression. While influential, Carson's approach diverges from Swiss Brutalism in its rejection of systematic order.

1	---
2	title: "Design Systems and Component Architecture for Web Applications"  
3	date: 2026-03-25  
4	summary: "Surveys the full landscape of design system practice for web applications—from Atomic Design methodology and design token standards to component API patterns, CSS architecture, accessibility, governance models, and AI-assisted generation—mapping major production systems including Spectrum, Carbon, Polaris, and Primer against the theoretical and tooling ecosystems that structure the field."  
5	keywords: [web-apps, design-systems, atomic-design, design-tokens, component-architecture]  
6	---
7	
8	# Design Systems and Component Architecture for Web Applications
9	
10	*March 2026*
11	
12	---
13	
14	## Abstract
15	
16	Design systems have evolved from style guide repositories into full infrastructure products that encode visual identity, behavioral contracts, accessibility requirements, governance processes, and token transformation pipelines into reusable component libraries. This survey examines the principal theoretical frameworks, technical architectures, tooling ecosystems, and production implementations that define the field as of early 2026. Topics covered include Brad Frost's Atomic Design methodology and its contemporary adaptations; the W3C Design Tokens Community Group specification (version 2025.10, the first stable release); multi-tier token layering for multi-brand and multi-theme architectures; component API design patterns including headless primitives, compound components, and slot-based composition; CSS architecture approaches ranging from utility-first frameworks to zero-runtime type-safe solutions; governance and versioning models; Figma-to-code pipeline maturity; documentation as product via Storybook; accessibility baked into component primitives; ROI measurement methodologies; and the emerging role of AI in token management and code generation. Five notable production systems—Adobe Spectrum, IBM Carbon, Shopify Polaris, GitHub Primer, and Salesforce Lightning—are examined as case studies. The survey identifies open problems including design-code parity gaps, cross-framework component interoperability, and the absence of standardized ROI measurement frameworks.
17	
18	---
19	
20	## 1. Introduction
21	
22	The term "design system" entered widespread professional use around 2015–2016, catalyzed by the publication of Brad Frost's *Atomic Design* methodology in 2013 and the subsequent proliferation of publicly documented systems from major technology organizations. Before this period, organizations maintained style guides—static documents specifying typography, color, and logo usage—but lacked mechanisms for synchronizing design decisions with production code. The design system concept united these concerns into a single managed artifact: a version-controlled collection of reusable components, design tokens, documentation, and governance processes that serves both designer and developer constituencies. [atomicdesign.bradfrost.com](https://atomicdesign.bradfrost.com/)
23	
24	The economic logic of design systems rests on the principle of "added once, used everywhere." A button component built once to specification, with accessibility, keyboard interaction, and theming handled internally, eliminates the need for each product team to rebuild that functionality independently. Rastelli's empirical analysis of design system adoption at Badoo demonstrated a clear reduction in CSS file change volume following system adoption, consistent with the theoretical prediction that system-mediated consistency reduces redundant code. [didoo.medium.com](https://didoo.medium.com/measuring-the-impact-of-a-design-system-7f925af090f7) Figma's 2024 ROI research reports average efficiency improvements of 38% for design teams and 31% for development teams following design system adoption. [figma.com](https://www.figma.com/reports/measure-design-system-roi/)
25	
26	The field has reached an infrastructure inflection point. The W3C Design Tokens Community Group published the first stable version of the Design Tokens Specification (2025.10) in October 2025, establishing a vendor-neutral JSON format for cross-tool token exchange. [designtokens.org](https://www.designtokens.org/) Figma has extended its design-development handoff capabilities with Dev Mode, Code Connect, Library Analytics API, and an MCP server for AI-assisted code generation. Major component libraries have bifurcated into headless behavior primitives (Radix UI, React Aria) and composition-forward patterns (shadcn/ui), while CSS architecture is diversifying across utility-first frameworks, zero-runtime type-safe solutions, and CSS Modules. Governance models have matured from centralized team ownership to federated contribution systems.
27	
28	This survey is organized as follows. Section 2 establishes the theoretical and historical foundations. Section 3 provides a taxonomy of approaches across the major dimensions of the field. Section 4 analyzes each dimension in depth. Section 5 provides a comparative synthesis. Section 6 identifies open problems. Section 7 concludes.
29	
30	---
31	
32	## 2. Foundations
33	
34	### 2.1 The Style Guide Era and Its Limitations
35	
36	Before design systems, organizations relied on static style guides—PDFs, wikis, or HTML pages—specifying brand colors, typography scales, and iconography. These artifacts suffered from fundamental maintenance problems: they described the intended design but had no mechanism for ensuring that production code reflected those intentions. Design drift—the gradual divergence between specified design and implemented product—was endemic, and it compounded with organizational scale. Each product team maintained its own component implementations, creating duplicated effort, inconsistent behavior, and accessibility gaps that varied by team.
37	
38	The Styleguide movement of the early 2010s, associated with practitioners such as Anna Debenham and Brad Frost, recognized that style guides needed to move closer to production code. Pattern libraries—collections of coded HTML/CSS components—represented a step toward what would become design systems, but they still lacked the token infrastructure, governance frameworks, and tooling pipelines that characterize mature systems. [atomicdesign.bradfrost.com](https://atomicdesign.bradfrost.com/chapter-1/)
39	
40	### 2.2 Atomic Design Methodology
41	
42	Brad Frost introduced Atomic Design in a blog post in 2013, later formalized in the book *Atomic Design* (2016). The methodology draws an analogy to chemistry, proposing that interfaces are composed of hierarchical units that combine according to structural rules: atoms (basic HTML elements that cannot be decomposed further while remaining functional), molecules (simple groups of atoms bonded together as a reusable UI component), organisms (complex assemblages of molecules and atoms forming distinct interface sections), templates (page-level layouts showing component arrangement and underlying content structure), and pages (concrete instances populated with real representative content). [atomicdesign.bradfrost.com/chapter-2](https://atomicdesign.bradfrost.com/chapter-2/)
43	
44	The methodology's key insight is that designers and developers must simultaneously work at multiple scales—both the granular detail of an individual input field and the holistic composition of a full page layout. This dual perspective, Frost argues, prevents isolated component design from failing to cohere at full scale. The hierarchical labeling provides a shared vocabulary across design and engineering teams.
45	
46	By 2025, Atomic Design has been widely adopted as a conceptual framework but is applied with pragmatic flexibility rather than strict adherence. Practitioners increasingly organize component directories by domain and dependency rather than by hierarchical level—a reflection that the atom/molecule/organism distinction, while conceptually useful, does not always map cleanly to real component boundaries. Extensions to the original five levels, including design tokens as a "foundation" layer and "ions" as sub-atomic units, reflect the model's evolution from rigid taxonomy to flexible metaphor. [medium.com/design-bootcamp](https://medium.com/design-bootcamp/atomic-design-in-2025-from-rigid-theory-to-flexible-practice-91f7113b9274) [dev.to](https://dev.to/m_midas/atomic-design-and-its-relevance-in-frontend-in-2025-32e9)
47	
48	### 2.3 Design Tokens: Origins and Standardization
49	
50	The concept of design tokens—named, platform-agnostic representations of design decisions—was pioneered by the Salesforce Lightning Design System team, which introduced the term in the mid-2010s to describe the CSS custom property values and Sass variables that encode color, spacing, typography, and other visual attributes. The insight was that these values, if given semantic names and managed in a central repository, could be transformed into platform-specific outputs (CSS custom properties, iOS Swift constants, Android resource files) through an automated pipeline. Theo, Salesforce's internal token tool, was eventually superseded by Amazon's open-source Style Dictionary, which became the community standard for token transformation pipelines. [styledictionary.com](https://styledictionary.com/info/dtcg/)
51	
52	The W3C Design Tokens Community Group (DTCG), established as a W3C Community Group, formalized the effort to standardize how design tokens are defined and exchanged across tools. The group released the first stable Design Tokens Specification (version 2025.10) in October 2025, establishing a JSON-based format with a typed value system. The specification provides cross-tool interoperability through standardized JSON formatting, support for theming, modern color spaces, and composite token types. Major design platforms including Adobe, Figma, Sketch, and Framer participate in the group and have committed to DTCG-compatible token formats. [w3.org/community/design-tokens](https://www.w3.org/community/design-tokens/) [designtokens.org](https://www.designtokens.org/)
53	
54	---
55	
56	## 3. Taxonomy of Approaches
57	
58	### Table 1. Taxonomy of design system dimensions and architectural approaches
59	
60	| Dimension | Approach variants | Representative implementations |
61	|---|---|---|
62	| Component hierarchy model | Atomic Design 5-level; domain-organized; feature-sliced | Brad Frost's methodology; most production systems informally |
63	| Token architecture | Single-tier flat; two-tier (primitive/semantic); three-tier (primitive/semantic/component) | Carbon v11 layering; Polaris token structure; Brad Frost's three-tier |
64	| Token format | Proprietary JSON; Style Dictionary config; W3C DTCG 2025.10 | Tokens Studio; Style Dictionary v4 |
65	| Token tooling | Style Dictionary; Tokens Studio; Theo (legacy) | amzn/style-dictionary; tokens.studio |
66	| CSS architecture | CSS-in-JS (runtime); CSS-in-JS (zero-runtime); CSS Modules; Utility-first; Vanilla CSS | Styled-components; vanilla-extract; CSS Modules; Tailwind v4 |
67	| Component library model | Fully styled; Headless/unstyled; Copy-paste styled; Web Components | MUI; Radix UI; shadcn/ui; Shoelace |
68	| Composition pattern | Props-based; Compound components; Slot-based; Render props; Headless hooks | React Spectrum; Radix; Headless UI |
69	| Governance model | Solitary; Centralized; Federated; Hybrid | Nathan Curtis's team models |
70	| Documentation tooling | Storybook; Docusaurus; Zeroheight; Supernova; Knapsack | Component library docs across the industry |
71	| Design-dev handoff | Figma Dev Mode; Code Connect; Zeplin; Abstract | Figma ecosystem |
72	| Accessibility approach | Bolted-on post-build; Baked-in primitive; Automated CI audit | React Aria; axe-core; ARIA APG |
73	| Testing strategy | Visual regression (Chromatic/Percy); a11y audit (axe); Snapshot; Unit | Storybook + Chromatic |
74	| Theming mechanism | CSS custom properties; CSS-in-JS theme provider; Token remapping | Material UI ThemeProvider; Tailwind config; Token layering |
75	| Multi-brand support | Token override files; Theme switching via alias tokens; CSS scope | Polaris, Signify/Photon case study |
76	| Monorepo tooling | Turborepo; Nx; Lerna + Nx | Large-scale design system monorepos |
77	| AI integration | Token generation; Figma MCP; Accessibility audits; Component scaffolding | Figma Make; UXPin Merge; Supernova |
78

4:[Omitted long matching line]
17:[Omitted long matching line]
155:| **Neo-Brutalist Expressionism** | Low-Medium (borrows vocabulary) | High (maximal rawness, visible mechanics) | Digital-native reinterpretation with emphasis on interface honesty | 2014-present |
165:[Omitted long matching line]
259:### 4.4 Neo-Brutalist Expressionism
263:[Omitted long matching line]
426:**Palette 2: High-Contrast Brutalist (Neo-Brutalist Expressionism)**
624:| Dimension | Orthodox Swiss Brutalism | Deconstructive Swiss Brutalism | Systematic Minimalism | Neo-Brutalist Expressionism |
645:| Digital products / apps | Neo-Brutalist Expressionism | Medium-appropriate, interface-honest |
647:| Counter-cultural / independent | Neo-Brutalist Expressionism | Anti-establishment signaling, low production barrier |
660:| 2014-present | Neo-Brutalist Expressionism proliferates | Digital-native brutalism; Brutalist Websites; mass-market adoption |
830:- **Brutalist Websites** (brutalistwebsites.com): Pascal Deville's curated collection of brutalist web design, the primary reference for Neo-Brutalist Expressionism in digital contexts.

259	### 4.4 Neo-Brutalist Expressionism
260	
261	#### 4.4.1 Theory
262	
263	Neo-Brutalist Expressionism is the most recent and most heterogeneous sub-approach. It applies brutalist principles specifically to digital environments, arguing that the dominant aesthetic of digital design -- smooth gradients, rounded corners, drop shadows, blur effects, animated transitions -- constitutes a form of material dishonesty equivalent to the applied ornament that Brutalism rejected in architecture. If a digital interface is built from HTML, CSS, and rectangular elements rendered by a pixel grid, then honest design should expose rather than conceal these realities.
264	
265	This position was articulated (though not in these terms) by Pascal Deville's Brutalist Websites project (2014-present), which collected examples of websites that rejected conventional polish. The theoretical discourse around Neo-Brutalism has since been developed in publications like *It's Nice That*, *Eye Magazine*, and various design criticism blogs.
266	
267	#### 4.4.2 Key Practitioners
268	
269	**Bloomberg Businessweek (under creative direction of Richard Turley, 2010-2014):** Turley's redesign introduced bold, raw, often confrontational graphic treatments to a mainstream business publication. The approach combined Swiss grid discipline with deliberately crude imagery, bold type, and high-contrast color -- a commercial application of Swiss Brutalist principles.
270	
271	**Virgil Abloh / Off-White (2012-2021):** Abloh's graphic work for Off-White and later Louis Vuitton borrowed heavily from Swiss Brutalist aesthetics: Helvetica in quotation marks, visible construction lines, raw typography treated as both text and image. While primarily a fashion phenomenon, Abloh's work brought Swiss Brutalist aesthetics to mass-market visibility.
272	
273	**Pascal Deville / Brutalist Websites (2014-present):** As curator rather than practitioner, Deville's collection project provided the taxonomy that consolidated "brutalist web design" as a recognized approach.
274	
275	**Balenciaga (under Demna Gvasalia, 2015-present):** The fashion house's digital presence and graphic identity employ deliberately raw, minimal, sometimes confrontational aesthetics that draw on both architectural Brutalism and Swiss typographic austerity.
276	
277	#### 4.4.3 Strengths and Limitations
278	
279	**Strengths:**
280	- Cultural relevance: addresses the specific conditions of contemporary digital design
281	- Democratic potential: the rejection of polish lowers the barrier to participation
282	- Critical force: makes visible the material conditions of digital production
283	
284	**Limitations:**
285	- Theoretical thinness: much Neo-Brutalist work is stylistic rather than principled
286	- Novelty dependence: the approach risks becoming a fashion that loses force through repetition
287	- Appropriation risk: brutalist aesthetics divorced from brutalist ethics become mere decoration
288	
289	---
290	
291	## 5. Design Specifications
292	
293	The following specifications synthesize the technical parameters of Swiss Brutalist Design across its sub-approaches. Where approaches diverge, distinctions are noted.
294	
295	### 5.1 Typography
296	
297	#### 5.1.1 Primary Typeface Families
298	
299	Swiss Brutalist Design draws from a restricted but precisely defined typographic palette:
300	
301	**Grotesque Sans-Serifs (Primary):**
302	- **Akzidenz-Grotesk** (Berthold, 1896): The original grotesque sans-serif and the typeface of the early Swiss Style. Preferred for its slight irregularities and humanist qualities compared to later neo-grotesques. Weights: Light, Regular, Medium, Bold, Super. Distinguishing features: double-story "a" and "g" in some weights, slightly condensed proportions.
303	- **Helvetica / Neue Helvetica** (Linotype, 1957/1983): The canonical Swiss typeface. In brutalist practice, Helvetica is used deliberately and self-consciously -- not as a neutral default but as a statement of commitment to the Swiss tradition. Neue Helvetica's rationalized weight system (25 Ultra Light through 95 Black) provides systematic control. Experimental Jetset's exclusive use of Helvetica is the definitive example.
304	- **Univers** (Adrian Frutiger, 1957): Frutiger's systematically designed superfamily, with its numbered weight/width naming system (Univers 55 = Roman, 65 = Bold, 45 = Light, etc.), embodies the programmatic approach central to Swiss Brutalism. The family's 27 original variants allow extreme precision in typographic hierarchy.
305	- **Neue Haas Grotesk** (Christian Schwartz, 2010): A revival that restores the original 1957 Haas Grotesk design intent, preferred by some practitioners over modern Helvetica for its warmer, less mechanized character.
306	
307	**Neo-Grotesque and Geometric Sans-Serifs (Secondary):**
308	- **Suisse Int'l** (Swiss Typefaces, 2011): A contemporary neo-grotesque designed explicitly for the Swiss tradition, with a comprehensive weight range and optical sizes.
309	- **Replica** (Norm, 2008): Designed by Norm specifically as an alternative to Helvetica, Replica is a "programmatic" sans-serif that exposes its own construction logic.
310	- **GT America** (Grilli Type, 2017): A Swiss-American synthesis that draws on both grotesque and gothic traditions, available in Compressed, Condensed, Standard, Extended, and Expanded widths.
311	- **Favorit** (Dinamo, 2016): A neo-grotesque with subtle personality, widely adopted in contemporary Swiss Brutalist work.
312	
313	**Monospaced Typefaces (Tertiary -- primarily Neo-Brutalist):**
314	- **Courier / Courier New** (Howard Kettler, 1955): The default monospace, used in Neo-Brutalist work for its associations with typewriters, terminals, and raw text.
315	- **GT America Mono** (Grilli Type, 2017): A monospaced companion designed within the Swiss tradition.
316	- **Suisse Mono** (Swiss Typefaces, 2011): Purpose-designed monospace within a Swiss neo-grotesque system.
317	- **SF Mono** (Apple, 2016): Used in Neo-Brutalist digital contexts for its association with code and system interfaces.
318	- **JetBrains Mono** (JetBrains, 2020): A developer-oriented monospace increasingly adopted in Neo-Brutalist design.
319	- **IBM Plex Mono** (IBM, 2017): A corporate monospace that bridges institutional and brutalist contexts.
320	
321	**Display and Accent Typefaces (Occasional):**
322	- **Druk** (Berton Hasebe / Commercial Type, 2014): An ultra-compressed to ultra-wide display family used for maximum typographic impact.
323	- **Knockout** (Hoefler & Co., 1994): A wide-ranging display sans-serif used in editorial Swiss Brutalist work.
324	- **Monument Extended** (Dinamo, 2017): An extended grotesque widely used in contemporary brutalist design.
325	- **Euclid** (Swiss Typefaces, 2015): A geometric sans-serif in the Swiss tradition.
326	
327	#### 5.1.2 Type Scale
328	
329	Swiss Brutalist Design employs a precise, mathematical type scale. The following specifications represent the canonical approach, with variations noted by sub-approach.
330	
331	**Base Unit and Scale Factor:**
332	
333	The traditional Swiss type scale derives from traditional typographic point sizes, organized in a proportional relationship. The primary scales used are:
334	
335	*Ratio-Based Scale (modular, following Muller-Brockmann):*
336	
337	| Role | Size (pt/px) | Scale Ratio | Usage |
338	|---|---|---|---|
339	| Micro caption | 8 / 10.67 | -- | Footnotes, captions, marginalia |
340	| Small body | 9 / 12 | 1.125x | Secondary body text, annotations |
341	| Body | 10 / 13.33 | 1.111x | Primary body text |
342	| Large body | 12 / 16 | 1.2x | Lead paragraphs, pullquotes |
343	| Subheading | 14 / 18.67 | 1.167x | Section subheadings |
344	| Heading 3 | 18 / 24 | 1.286x | Tertiary headings |
345	| Heading 2 | 24 / 32 | 1.333x | Secondary headings |
346	| Heading 1 | 36 / 48 | 1.5x | Primary headings |
347	| Display small | 48 / 64 | 1.333x | Small display text |
348	| Display medium | 72 / 96 | 1.5x | Medium display text |
349	| Display large | 96 / 128 | 1.333x | Large display text |
350	| Display XL | 144 / 192 | 1.5x | Maximum impact display |
351	
352	*Note: Point-to-pixel conversion assumes 96 PPI (1pt = 1.333px). Print specifications use points; screen specifications use pixels.*
353	
354	*Fibonacci-Adjacent Scale (used by some systematic practitioners):*
355	
356	8, 13, 16, 21, 34, 55, 89 (approximating the golden ratio 1.618 at each step)
357	
358	**Line Height (Leading):**

420	| Tertiary text | Medium grey | `#666666` | Metadata, timestamps, labels |
421	| Disabled/muted | Light grey | `#999999` | Inactive elements, placeholders |
422	| Subtle ground | Near-white | `#F5F5F5` | Section backgrounds, cards |
423	| Primary ground | White | `#FFFFFF` | Page background |
424	| Accent (single) | Signal red | `#FF0000` | Emphasis, links, critical markers |
425	
426	**Palette 2: High-Contrast Brutalist (Neo-Brutalist Expressionism)**
427	
428	| Role | Color | Hex | Usage |
429	|---|---|---|---|
430	| Primary text | True black | `#000000` | All text |
431	| Primary ground | True white | `#FFFFFF` | Page background |
432	| Primary accent | Pure red | `#FF0000` | Primary interactive elements |
433	| Secondary accent | Pure blue | `#0000FF` | Links, secondary actions |
434	| Tertiary accent | Pure yellow | `#FFFF00` | Highlights, warnings |
435	| Border / structure | Black | `#000000` | All borders, dividers, structural lines |
436	
437	**Palette 3: Material (Concrete / Architectural Reference)**
438	
439	| Role | Color | Hex | Usage |
440	|---|---|---|---|
441	| Primary text | Charcoal | `#1A1A1A` | Body text, headings |
442	| Primary ground | Warm concrete | `#E8E4E0` | Page background |
443	| Secondary ground | Cool concrete | `#D4D0CC` | Section backgrounds |
444	| Dark ground | Raw concrete | `#8C8985` | Footer, inverse sections |
445	| Accent warm | Oxide red | `#C0392B` | Primary accent |
446	| Accent cool | Steel blue | `#2C3E50` | Secondary accent |
447	| Highlight | Raw amber | `#D4A017` | Tertiary accent, highlights |
448	
449	**Palette 4: Extended Institutional (Systematic Minimalism)**
450	
451	| Role | Color | Hex | Usage |
452	|---|---|---|---|
453	| Primary text | Near-black | `#111111` | All primary text |
454	| Secondary text | Dark grey | `#555555` | Secondary text |
455	| Primary ground | Off-white | `#FAFAFA` | Page background |
456	| Accent 1 | Institutional blue | `#003DA5` | Primary accent |
457	| Accent 2 | Signal red | `#E50000` | Error / urgent / emphasis |
458	| Accent 3 | Dark green | `#006B3F` | Success / positive |
459	| Neutral | Mid grey | `#AAAAAA` | Borders, dividers |
460	
461	#### 5.2.3 Color Usage Ratios
462	
463	Following the architectural principle that structure dominates and color accents, Swiss Brutalist Design adheres to strict usage ratios:
464	
465	| Element | Proportion | Notes |
466	|---|---|---|
467	| Background (white/off-white/concrete) | 60-75% | The dominant field |
468	| Text (black/dark grey) | 15-25% | Text as primary content |
469	| Structural elements (borders, rules, grids) | 5-10% | Visible but subordinate |
470	| Accent color | 2-5% | Minimal, high-impact |
471	
472	**The 60-30-10 variant:** Some practitioners use a simplified ratio:
473	- 60% background
474	- 30% primary text and structure (black/dark grey)
475	- 10% accent (single color, maximum two)
476	
477	#### 5.2.4 Contrast Rules
478	
479	- **Minimum contrast ratio for body text:** 7:1 (WCAG AAA) -- Swiss Brutalist practice generally exceeds this significantly, as black-on-white achieves 21:1
480	- **Minimum contrast ratio for large text (18pt+ or 14pt+ bold):** 4.5:1
481	- **Accent color contrast:** Accent colors must achieve at least 3:1 against their background for non-text elements, 4.5:1 for text
482	- **Never use color alone to convey information** -- always pair with typographic or structural differentiation
483	
484	### 5.3 Grid & Layout
485	
486	#### 5.3.1 Grid Philosophy
487	
488	The grid is the foundational structural element of Swiss Brutalist Design. Unlike decorative approaches where the grid is invisible infrastructure, in Swiss Brutalist practice the grid may be visible -- either literally (printed/displayed grid lines) or phenomenologically (the grid's presence is felt through rigorous alignment).
489	
490	Josef Muller-Brockmann's formulation is canonical: the grid is a system of proportional relationships that organizes all elements in a composition according to a mathematical order. It is not a constraint but a framework for decision-making.
491	
492	#### 5.3.2 Column Systems
493	
494	**Standard Print Grids:**
495	
496	| System | Columns | Gutter | Margin (outer) | Margin (inner) | Application |
497	|---|---|---|---|---|---|
498	| Minimal | 2 | 12pt (4.23mm) | 48pt (16.93mm) | 48pt | Posters, single-focus layouts |
499	| Standard | 4 | 10pt (3.53mm) | 36pt (12.70mm) | 36pt | Books, catalogs, reports |

1	# Audio Safety Hardening
2	
3	## Goal
4	Verify and harden all audio safety invariants across the codebase. Check that:
5	1. The master bus hard limiter (INV-2) exists, is non-bypassable, and is the final node before destination
6	2. DFS cycle detection (INV-1) runs before EVERY AudioNode.connect() call -- including preset loads, session loads, and programmatic graph construction
7	3. Voice pool has a fixed ceiling (INV-5) with no runtime growth
8	4. AudioWorklet process() methods contain zero heap allocations (INV-3) -- no `new Array`, `new Float32Array`, no object creation
9	5. Transport stop guarantees silence within 10ms (INV-6)
10	6. Gain parameters are clamped to maximum +12dB
11	7. Emergency mute activates on sustained clipping (>500ms)
12	
13	After each check, fix any violations found. Output `IMPROVED` if fixes were made and all tests pass. Output `NO_IMPROVEMENT` if all invariants already hold. Output `FAILED` if fixes were attempted but tests fail.
14	
15	## Validation
16	- `pnpm check` passes (lint + type check)
17	- `pnpm test` passes (all existing tests)
18	- No AudioNode.connect() call exists without a preceding cycle detection check
19	- No `new` keyword appears in any AudioWorkletProcessor.process() method body
20	- Hard limiter node exists in the master bus chain with the correct DynamicsCompressorNode parameters
